patterns.title: 1. Empty Market Exchange Rate Inflation
patterns.rootCause: When a CompoundV2-fork cToken market has extremely low `totalSupply` (e.g., 2 shares), directly transferring the underlying token to the cToken contract inflates the `exchangeRate = totalCash / totalS

patterns.title: 2. Share Price Inflation via Donation
patterns.rootCause: Protocols that track asset balances using variables like `pseudoTotalPool` are vulnerable when the actual token balance can be inflated via direct `transfer()` without minting new shares. The ratio `a

patterns.title: Vulnerability Description
patterns.rootCause:
The fundamental issue stems from the share minting formula used in vault-based systems:



```solidity

shares = (depositAmount * totalShares) / totalAssets

```



When `totalAssets` is much larger t

patterns.title: Vulnerable Pattern Examples
patterns.rootCause: Standard ERC4626 without inflation protection

patterns.title: 1. First Depositor / Inflation Attack
patterns.rootCause: ERC4626 and similar vault implementations calculate shares as `assets * totalSupply / totalAssets`. When totalSupply is small (e.g., 1 share) and totalAssets is inflated via donation, this formula rou

patterns.title: 2. Exchange Rate Manipulation via Donation
patterns.rootCause: Vault exchange rates are calculated from on-chain balances that can be artificially inflated through direct token transfers. Functions that allow "donations" without proper access control can be abuse

patterns.title: 3. Reward Distribution Edge Cases
patterns.rootCause: Reward-per-token calculations use `lastUpdated` timestamps that aren't updated when `totalSupply == 0`, causing reward accumulation to be retroactively applied to the first staker.

patterns.title: 4. Stale Reward Accumulator State
patterns.rootCause: Rewards not updated before stake change

patterns.title: 5. Flash Loan LP Fee Extraction
patterns.rootCause: No timelock between deposits and withdrawals allows attackers to front-run legitimate transactions with flash-loaned deposits, claiming a share of fees for minimal risk.

patterns.title: 6. Cross-Function Reentrancy in Token Hooks
patterns.rootCause: Hooks read state at start of transfer but external calls allow that state to be modified before the hook completes processing, causing double-counting or missed updates.

patterns.title: 7. Read-Only Reentrancy in Oracle Integration
patterns.rootCause: Balancer (and similar AMMs) update BPT supply before updating token balances during joins. Read-only reentrancy allows reading supply after update but balances before, causing price miscalculation.

patterns.title: 8. Partial Withdrawal Token Freeze
patterns.rootCause: Provider contracts transfer full requested share amount from user but underlying withdrawal may only consume partial shares (due to liquidity constraints). Remaining shares stay in provider with no re

patterns.title: 9. Reward Multiplication via Merge Operations
patterns.rootCause: Claimable rewards are calculated based on current balance at claim time. Merging positions increases balance of destination, allowing it to claim rewards based on combined balance even though source a

patterns.title: 10. State Machine Hijacking
patterns.rootCause: State machines allow transitions from completed/error states back to initial states, and the function allowing re-initialization doesn't verify caller is the original owner.

patterns.title: 11. Penalty and Fee Bypass via Secondary Markets
patterns.rootCause: transfer() and transferFrom() functions not disabled or overridden to apply penalties/fees, allowing penalty-free exit via secondary market sales.

patterns.title: 12. Claim Function Arithmetic Underflow
patterns.rootCause: After the first user claims post-period, `lastUpdateTime` is set beyond `periodFinish`. Subsequent calculations of `min(block.timestamp, periodFinish) - lastUpdateTime` underflow.

patterns.title: 13. Missing Slippage Protection in Yield Operations
patterns.rootCause: No minimum output specified

patterns.title: 14. Incorrect Share Calculation on Edge Cases
patterns.rootCause: Division by zero possible

patterns.title: 15. Reward Lockup on Period Transitions
patterns.rootCause: Old rewards potentially lost

patterns.title: 16. Vault Accounting Desync
patterns.rootCause: Doesn't account for rebases

patterns.title: 17. External Call Context Confusion
patterns.rootCause: `this.function()` makes an external call with the contract as msg.sender instead of the original caller, bypassing intended access controls.

patterns.title: 18. Deposit/Withdrawal Same-Block Arbitrage
patterns.rootCause: Same block operations allowed

patterns.title: 19. Strategy Migration Token Loss
patterns.rootCause: May leave tokens in old strategy

patterns.title: 20. Compound Interest Manipulation
patterns.rootCause: No limit on compound frequency

patterns.title: 21. Time-Weighted Voting Power Exploitation
patterns.rootCause: Can lock and vote in same transaction

patterns.title: 22. Emergency Withdrawal Accounting Errors
patterns.rootCause: Accounting not updated

patterns.title: 23. Yield Aggregator Fund Isolation Failures
patterns.rootCause: All vaults share same strategy balance

patterns.title: 24. Vote Manipulation via Duplicate Pool Entries
patterns.rootCause: Vote function doesn't validate that pools in the array are unique. It stores only the last weight but increments totalWeight for each entry, allowing the difference to accumulate with reset cycles.

patterns.title: 25. Vesting Contract Interface Spoofing
patterns.rootCause: Claim function accepts any address as "DAO contract" and reads token address, contribution amounts, and vesting schedules from it without validating the contract is legitimate.

patterns.title: 26. Unbounded Reward Accrual After Period End
patterns.rootCause: getRewardPerToken() continues calculating rewards when `lastUpdateTime < periodFinish` regardless of whether `block.timestamp > periodFinish`, causing rewards to keep accumulating.

patterns.title: 27. Precision Library Mismatch
patterns.rootCause: Rate is set using PreciseMathUtils (10^27 divisor) but calculated using MathUtils (10^6 divisor), causing result to be 10^21 times larger than expected.

patterns.title: 28. Unbounded Loop DoS via Array Growth
patterns.rootCause: vest() allows anyone to create vestments for any beneficiary without minimum amount. claim() loops through all timelocks. Attacker can push millions of tiny vestments.

patterns.title: 29. Minimum Deposit Bypass via Withdrawal
patterns.rootCause: Deposit function checks MIN_INITIAL_DEPOSIT when totalSupply == 0, but withdraw doesn't prevent reducing supply below minimum, enabling return to vulnerable state.

patterns.title: 30. Strategy Migration State Loss
patterns.rootCause: setInterestRateStrategy() replaces strategy contract but new contract has zeroed vault-specific parameters (targetUtilization, lastUpdate, etc).

patterns.title: 31. Double Subtraction Accounting Error
patterns.rootCause: Function retrieves totalStaked which already has totalFrozen subtracted, then subtracts totalFrozen again, resulting in double deduction.

patterns.title: 32. Reentrancy Lock Bypass via Storage Mode Switch
patterns.rootCause: Lock set in regular storage before activation. Attacker triggers storage mode switch mid-call. Inner call checks transient storage (empty). Lock bypassed, reentrancy enabled.

patterns.title: 33. Timestamp Boundary Condition in Activity Checks
patterns.rootCause: `_wasActiveAt()` returns true when `disabledTime >= timestamp`. At exact equality (disabled at epoch start), entity should be inactive but is counted as active.

patterns.title: 34. First Depositor Market Bricking
patterns.rootCause: Utilization rate formula allows results > 100% when `borrows > cash + borrows - reserves`. Interest rate calculations use unbounded utilization, exceeding maximums.

patterns.title: 35. Collateral Rebalancing Gaps
patterns.rootCause: setRatio() changes target allocation but only affects new deposits. Existing TVL remains at old ratio with no rebalancing function.

patterns.title: Vulnerable Pattern Examples
patterns.rootCause: Farm credits `amount` deposited, not actual tokens received

```

amount_sent == amount_received

```



For fee-on-transfer tokens:

```

amount_received = amount_sent - fee

```



When protocols use `amount_sent

function _transfer(address from, address to, uint256 amount) intern

patterns.title: 6. Low/No Decimals Token Issues

patterns.title: 7. Pausable/Blacklistable Token Risks
patterns.rootCause: No handling for paused tokens

patterns.title: 3. LiquidityIndex Inflation via Recursive Flash Loans
patterns.rootCause: Aave V2 fork lending pools that allow flash loans from their own pool create a recursive amplification vector. Each flash loan from the pool accumulates a premium that inflates the `liquidityIndex`. A

patterns.title: 4. Scaling Factor Precision Loss in StableMath
patterns.rootCause: Balancer V2 ComposableStablePools use scaling factors to normalize token amounts with different decimals or wrapped token rates (e.g., wstETH, osETH). The `FixedPoint.mulDown(amount, scalingFactor)` o

patterns.title: 5. Share Price Inflation via Direct Contract Donation
patterns.rootCause: When vault or lending protocols use `totalAssets() / totalShares()` for share pricing and rely on the contract's token balance for `totalAssets()`, an attacker can donate tokens directly to the underl

patterns.title: 6. Virtual Balance Math Manipulation
patterns.rootCause: Multi-asset pools using virtual balance models (e.g., yETH with 8 LST assets) track invariants via `vb_prod` and `vb_sum` variables. When rate providers (like OETH rebase) change asset rates mid-manip

patterns.title: Category 1: Division Precision Loss (Rounding Down)
patterns.rootCause: Solidity integer division always rounds toward zero (truncates). When division is performed before multiplication, or when scaling factors cause small amounts to truncate to zero, precision is permane

patterns.title: Category 2: Exchange Rate Manipulation via Donation (Inflation Attacks)
patterns.rootCause: When protocols calculate exchange rates using `totalAssets / totalShares`, attackers can donate assets directly to the contract to inflate the exchange rate, causing subsequent depositors to receive f

patterns.title: Category 3: CompoundV2/ERC4626 Vault Inflation Attacks
patterns.rootCause:
CompoundV2-style protocols and ERC4626 vaults are vulnerable when:

1. The market/vault is empty or nearly empty

2. An attacker can be the first depositor

3. Share minting uses `assets * totalShares

patterns.title: Category 4: Integer Truncation Issues
patterns.rootCause: Direct integer truncation occurs when calculations involve division operations where the result should be non-zero but truncates to zero, or when intermediate calculations lose significant bits.

patterns.title: Category 5: First Depositor Attacks
patterns.rootCause: When a vault or pool is empty, the first depositor can manipulate the initial share price through strategic deposits and donations, affecting all subsequent depositors.

patterns.title: Category 1: Reward/Staking Calculation Errors
patterns.rootCause:
Reward calculations that depend on:

1. External balances (e.g., pair/pool balances) rather than tracked internal state

2. Time-based rewards without proper epoch/checkpoint management

3. Claimable

patterns.title: Category 2: Dividend Distribution Issues
patterns.rootCause:
Dividend/profit-sharing mechanisms that can be gamed through:

1. Donation attacks that inflate dividend-per-share

2. Immediate claim after large donations

3. Dividend calculation based on manipulab

patterns.title: Category 3: Integer Overflow/Underflow
patterns.rootCause:
Arithmetic operations without proper bounds checking:

1. Pre-Solidity 0.8.0 contracts without SafeMath

2. Unchecked blocks in Solidity 0.8.0+

3. Subtraction before checking balance

patterns.title: Category 4: AMM K-Value Manipulation
patterns.rootCause:
AMM pools that don't properly validate the constant product invariant (k = x * y):

1. Weak k-value checks that can be bypassed

2. Sync operations that update reserves without validating invariant

3

patterns.title: Category 5: Transfer Logic Calculation Errors
patterns.rootCause:
Deflationary tokens with transfer fees/burns that have flawed calculations:

1. Burns during transfer that affect pair balances incorrectly

2. Fee calculations that can be exploited with specific amo

patterns.title: Category 6: Fee Calculation Bugs
patterns.rootCause:
Incorrect fee calculations in:

1. Swap fee math that rounds incorrectly

2. Tax tokens with flawed percentage calculations

3. Rebasing tokens where fee applies before/after rebase incorrectly

subsections.title: Fix 1: Internal Balance Tracking for Rewards

patterns.title: Root Cause
patterns.rootCause:
In Solidity < 0.8.0, arithmetic operations silently overflow/underflow:

- `uint256(2) - uint256(3) = 2^256 - 1` (underflow → max value)

- `uint256(2^255) * 2 = 0` (overflow → wraps to zero)



If a

patterns.title: 3. Unprotected burnFrom + transferFrom (DEI $5.4M)

patterns.rootCause: BNO's staking pool had an `emergencyWithdraw()` function that reset a user's staked token balance but did not reclaim or lock the staked NFTs. After calling `emergencyWithdraw()`, the user could still

patterns.title: 6. Flash Loan Liquidity Manipulation (Palmswap $900K)
patterns.rootCause: Palmswap's liquidity event contract allowed users to purchase PLP tokens at a 1:1 ratio with USDP, but the withdrawal ratio was calculated based on the pool's current USDP reserves. By depositing a la

patterns.title: 7. convertDustToEarned Price Manipulation (BEARNDAO $769K)
patterns.rootCause: BEARNDAO's Bvaults strategy had a public `convertDustToEarned()` function that swapped residual "dust" tokens to the earned token via a DEX. An attacker could front-run this call by first buying the e

patterns.rootCause: Platypus Finance's lending system allowed users to borrow the protocol's stablecoin (USP) against staked LP tokens. The protocol did not properly account for flash-loan-inflated positions during the b

patterns.title: 2. Repeated Withdrawal Without State Invalidation

patterns.title: 1. Missing Solvency Check on Donation Functions
patterns.rootCause: Euler Finance's `donateToReserves()` function allowed users to move their eTokens (collateral) to the protocol's reserves without checking whether the account remained solvent after the donation. By d

patterns.title: 2. Precision Loss in Concentrated Liquidity Tick Math
patterns.rootCause: KyberSwap's concentrated liquidity implementation had numeric precision errors in the tick-crossing calculation. When an attacker carefully positioned the pool price at a range boundary with zero liqu

patterns.title: 3. Internal vs Actual Balance Divergence
patterns.rootCause: ElasticSwap tracked internal balances (`baseTokenReserveQty`, `quoteTokenReserveQty`, `kLast`) separately from actual ERC20 token balances. When tokens were directly transferred to the exchange contra

patterns.title: 4. Unchecked Swap Router Callback Trust
patterns.rootCause: SushiSwap's RouteProcessor2 accepted arbitrary `route` bytes that could specify any contract address as a "pool" for swap callbacks. When the router called `swap()` on the attacker's fake "pool" contr

patterns.title: 1. Freeze Authority Not Revoked After Pool Creation
patterns.rootCause: `revoke_freeze_authority` function exists in `locker.rs` but is never called during pool creation, leaving the bonding curve program as freeze authority.

patterns.title: 2. Unvalidated Edition in SAM create() Drains All Funds
patterns.rootCause: `create()` accepts any address as an edition without verifying it's a legitimate `SoundEdition` contract. Malicious contracts bypass all modifier checks.

patterns.title: 3. Reentrancy During Cred Creation Steals All Ether
patterns.rootCause: No reentrancy guard on `createCred`, `buyShareCred`, `sellShareCred`. The `credIdCounter` is incremented after external calls, allowing the attacker to re-enter and create a new cred that overwrites t

patterns.title: 4. Denom Change While Active Bids Exist
patterns.rootCause: No check for active bids when changing denom

patterns.title: 5. Missing Access Control on Oracle/Manager Address Setters
patterns.rootCause: No access control modifier

patterns.title: 6. Unprotected Orderbook Functions Enable Manipulation
patterns.rootCause: No access control — bypasses matching engine

patterns.title: 7. Golden Egg State Drift via Non-Frozen editionMaxMintable
patterns.rootCause: editionMaxMintable keeps growing with SAM mints

patterns.title: 8. Inconsistent Bond Curve Update Breaks Deposit Accounting
patterns.rootCause: updateBondCurve changes unbonded keys but not depositable keys

patterns.title: 9. Race Condition Between Buyer and Owner Withdrawal
patterns.rootCause: No check if curve already completed before pausing

patterns.title: 10. Registry Admin Can Override Upgrade Contract
patterns.rootCause: Owner can override any entry including the upgrader

patterns.title: 11. Converter isActive Before Full Configuration
patterns.rootCause: Returns true before configuration is complete

patterns.title: 13. Frontrunning reduceWeight via Marketcap Manipulation
patterns.rootCause: Marketcap easily manipulable via spot reserves

patterns.title: 14. Force-Sent Native Token DOS Graduation
patterns.rootCause: Uses raw balance instead of tracked amount

patterns.title: 1. Fee Calculated on Wrong Base Amount
patterns.rootCause: The fee is `(ethNeeded * feeBps) / 10000` which gives the fee *on the pre-fee amount*. But `totalCost = ethNeeded + fee` means the user pays less total than they should, since `fee / totalCost < feeBp

patterns.title: 2. Last Buy Fee Mismatch on Bonding Curve
patterns.rootCause: Fee calculated before actual amount is known

patterns.title: 3. Swap Fee Omitted in Order Reflections
patterns.rootCause: Missing (1-f) multiplier on bid/ask sizing

patterns.title: 4. Inconsistent Fee Between Quoter and Execution
patterns.rootCause: Quoter adds hook fee but execution doesn't

patterns.title: 5. Expansion Mint Precision Loss Causes DOS
patterns.rootCause: newRatio computed at 1e18, stored at 1e8 precision

patterns.title: 6. Root/Exponent Library Truncation Compounds Error
patterns.rootCause: `twoThirdsRoot(x)` computes `cubeRoot(x)^2` — the truncation in `cubeRoot` is squared. Similarly, `threeHalfsRoot(x)` computes `sqrt(x)^3` — truncation is cubed. Both propagate large downward bias int

patterns.title: 7. Invariant Rounding Direction Mismatch
patterns.rootCause: Notional rounds up, Balancer always rounds down

patterns.title: 8. Underpriced Quote via Integer Division Truncation
patterns.rootCause: Integer division always truncates — favors buyer

patterns.title: 9. Invariant Check Invalidated by Rent Inclusion
patterns.rootCause: lamports() includes rent, real_sol_reserves does not

patterns.title: 10. Fee Reward Dust Permanently Stuck in Contract
patterns.rootCause: Division truncation creates permanent dust

patterns.title: 11. Fee-on-Transfer Token Accounting Mismatch
patterns.rootCause: Assumes full amount is received

patterns.title: 1. Oracle Price Denomination Mismatch (USD vs DAI)
patterns.rootCause: All oracles (WETH, WBTC, DAI, USDC) return USD price, but the stablecoin targets DAI peg. `getOwnValuation()` divides by the DAI/USD rate, which is imprecise and inverted during depegs.

patterns.title: 2. Stale TWAP Oracle Due to Unpoked Metapool
patterns.rootCause: Reads stale cumulative balances from metapool

patterns.title: 3. Flash Loan Protection Bypass via Self-Liquidation
patterns.rootCause: `deposit()` sets `idToBlockOfLastDeposit[id] = block.number`. `withdraw()` checks this. But `liquidate → vault.move(id, to, amount)` doesn't update `idToBlockOfLastDeposit[to]`, so the recipient accou

patterns.title: 4. Rebalance Rate Limiting Missing — Vault Drainage
patterns.rootCause: ~1% slippage per call, but unlimited calls per transaction

patterns.title: 5. Wash Trading to Steal Keeper/Spot Trading Rewards
patterns.rootCause: `closePositionByCondition()` accepts `CLOSE_BY_TRADER` as a close reason. Activity tracking increments on open and doesn't decrement on close. Rewards are distributed proportionally to `traderActivity

patterns.title: 6. Batch Operation Fails Atomically on Single Asset
patterns.rootCause: Single failure reverts the entire batch

patterns.title: 7. Unsafe Downcast Breaks Supply Invariant
patterns.rootCause: Supply > 2^128 overflows silently

patterns.title: 8. Collateral Depeg Cascading to Bonding Curve
patterns.rootCause: Assumes 1 DAI = $1 always

patterns.title: 9. Asymmetric Base/Quote Treatment in Virtual Bonding Curve
patterns.rootCause: Fee applied to base on buy, but to quote on sell

patterns.title: 10. Protocol Fees Stuck After Graduation
patterns.rootCause: Fees locked after graduation

patterns.title: 11. Token Supply Not Correctly Burned on Graduation
patterns.rootCause: Unsold tokens not burned during graduation

patterns.title: 12. Graduation Stuck Due to Third-Party Contract Interference
patterns.rootCause: If Uniswap router call fails, graduation is permanently stuck

patterns.title: Vulnerability Description
patterns.rootCause:
The fundamental issues across all 10 findings:



1. **No slippage parameters at all** — Buy/sell functions accept no `minAmountOut` or `maxAmountIn`, leaving users completely unprotected (Reports 1,

patterns.title: Vulnerable Pattern Examples
patterns.rootCause: No slippage parameter — price depends on current tokenCount

subsections.title: **Pattern 1: No Slippage on Bonding Curve Share Buy/Sell** [MEDIUM]
**Pattern 3: Missing Slippage on Credit Token Sells** [MEDIUM]
**Pattern 4: Zero Slippage in Internal UniswapV2 Calls** [MEDIUM]
**Pattern 5: Sandwich on UniswapV3 Liquidity Lock Position Conversion** [HIGH]
**Pattern 6: No Slippage on Bonding Curve Market Seat Operations** [MEDIUM]
**Pattern 7: Slippage Not Enforced During Token Graduation** [MEDIUM]
**Pattern 8: Liquidity Amount Calculated from Manipulable On-Chain Price** [HIGH]
**Pattern 9: No Slippage or Deadline During Stability Burns** [MEDIUM]
**Pattern 10: Slippage Check Before Graduation Supply Cap** [MEDIUM]

patterns.title: 1. Premature Launch via Default Value Bypass
patterns.rootCause: The `commitLaunch[tokenId]` defaults to 0, so the check `block.number <= commitLaunch[tokenId]` always passes for uninitialized tokens—any block number > 0 passes the condition.

patterns.title: 2. Flash Loan Genesis / Graduation Exploit
patterns.rootCause: All operations (deposit → launch → redeem → sell) can execute atomically in a single transaction with no time separation between launch and redemption.

patterns.title: 3. Flash Loan Premature Graduation with Atomic Unwrap
patterns.rootCause: `unwrapToken()` has no time-based restrictions—it allows converting memecoin to agentToken atomically upon graduation, closing the flash loan loop.

patterns.title: 4. New Bonding Curve Launch Volatility Arbitrage
patterns.rootCause: New bonding curve offers discounted tokens (e.g., 5% off), and flash loans can atomically buy discounted tokens and sell on existing DEX pools for profit.

patterns.title: 5. Griefing UniswapV3 Launch by Pre-Creating Pool
patterns.rootCause: `launchGroupCoinToUniswapV3()` calls `uniswapV3Factory.createPool()` which reverts if the pool already exists, and there's no fallback to `getPool()`.

patterns.title: 6. CREATE2 Pair Address Mismatch Permanently Stuck
patterns.rootCause: The factory's salt includes `(token0, token1, launchpadLp, launchpadFeeDistributor)` but `pairFor()` only uses `(token0, token1)`.

patterns.title: 7. Early Pair Creation Breaks Fair Launch
patterns.rootCause: No restriction on creating a DEX pair for the token before graduation. The liquidity addition uses the existing pair's reserves to calculate token amounts.

patterns.title: 8. Donation Guard Bypass via Wrong Pair Address
patterns.rootCause: pairFor() returns wrong address, so check always passes

patterns.title: 9. Front-Running createPair Breaks Bootstrap
patterns.rootCause: If pair exists, falls through to AMM logic instead of bootstrap

patterns.title: 10. Double-Stake Migration Locks Founder Tokens
patterns.rootCause: No mechanism to unlock old LP tokens during migration

patterns.title: 11. Presale Tokens Grant Premature Voting Power
patterns.rootCause: Directly minting tokens during presale grants immediate governance power

patterns.title: 12. Dutch Auction Price Manipulation
patterns.rootCause: `_endAuction()` only triggers when commitments >= maxCommitments. Purchasing 1 wei shy prevents finalization, and the remaining 1 wei purchase will revert in AMMs due to rounding.

patterns.title: 13. Crowdfund Rug Pull via Zero MaxPrice NFT Listing
patterns.rootCause: maximumPrice of 0 allows any NFT to be "purchased"

patterns.title: 4. Exchange Rate Calculation Errors

patterns.title: 5. Delegation Enforcer Bypasses

patterns.title: 1. Sandwich / Front-Running Reward Claims

patterns.title: 2. msg.sender Confusion in Reward Re-Staking

patterns.title: 3. Reward Accounting Double-Counting
patterns.rootCause: This vulnerability exists because **delayed effective balance and delayed rewards are assumed to be claimed between reporting cycles**. If unclaimed, the next report's `reportSweptBalance` counts them

patterns.title: 4. Stale Snapshot Data After Unstaking

patterns.rootCause: This vulnerability exists because **`rewardValidators()` distributes rewards proportionally to all current stakes without any time-weighting**, and there is no cooldown on staking/unstaking that would

patterns.title: 2. Over-Slashing and Double-Reduction
patterns.rootCause: This vulnerability exists because **slashing accounting applies reductions in multiple places without coordination**. After `_transferToSlashStore` reduces `totalRestakedETH`, the subsequent `validate

patterns.title: 3. Slashing Bypass via Timing
patterns.rootCause: This vulnerability exists because **over-commitment proofs reduce shares to 0, and slashing 0 shares has no effect**. After the operator is unfrozen, the staker exits the beacon chain and gets full sh

patterns.title: 4. Protocol Insolvency from LST Slashing
patterns.rootCause: This vulnerability exists because **reward tracking functions floor negative returns at zero** (`max(0, balance - strategy)`), and withdrawal accounting assumes `received == requested`. During a Lido

patterns.title: 5. Unfair Penalty Distribution
patterns.rootCause: This vulnerability exists because **settlement functions use `balanceBefore`/`balanceAfter` patterns** that capture any accumulated deficit. The first cohort to settle an epoch absorbs the entire slas

patterns.title: 6. Slashing Accounting Errors
patterns.rootCause: Slashing reduces balance metrics but recovery doesn't increase them

patterns.title: 1. Withdrawal Queue Manipulation
patterns.rootCause: This vulnerability exists because **epoch/queue state transitions are not atomic** with EigenLayer's settlement process. When the settlement epoch is not incremented immediately upon initiation, or wh

patterns.title: 2. Share/Amount Calculation Errors During Withdrawal
patterns.rootCause: This vulnerability exists because **share-to-asset conversions are performed at a fixed point in time** (request time) without accounting for value changes during the withdrawal delay. Additionally, r

patterns.title: 3. Fund Lockup During Withdrawal
patterns.rootCause: This vulnerability exists because **withdrawal flows assume ideal execution conditions** — strategies always have withdrawal functions, ETH transfers always succeed, external protocols always return e

patterns.title: 4. Slashing Interaction with Withdrawals
patterns.rootCause: This vulnerability exists because **mid-withdrawal slashing invalidates the withdrawal root** used to claim. When `slashQueuedWithdrawal` is called between initiation and claim, the withdrawal root is

patterns.title: 5. Withdrawal Delay Bypass
patterns.rootCause: This vulnerability exists because **withdrawal delays are either recalculable, uninitialized after upgrades, or not updated on retry**. Weighted-average lockup recalculation allows dilution via large

patterns.title: 6. Missing Slippage/Deadline Protection
patterns.rootCause: No minimum output or deadline

patterns.title: 7. Partial Failure Handling
patterns.rootCause: 63/64 gas rule allows targeted failure

patterns.title: 8. Reentrancy and Access Control in Withdrawal Flows
patterns.rootCause: update() callable during swap callback

patterns.title: 1. Liquidation Mechanism Failures
patterns.rootCause: Liquidation functions either force full position closure (blocking whale liquidation), allow the position holder to profit from their own liquidation, or can be DoS'd by frontrunning.

patterns.title: 2. Funding Rate Vulnerabilities
patterns.rootCause: Funding rate is calculated from a subset of the market (e.g., one maker's skew) but applied globally, or the rate direction is inverted, or payments are denominated in the wrong token.

patterns.title: 3. Position & Leverage Accounting Bugs
patterns.rootCause: Integer division precision loss in margin/leverage calculations, or missing state updates when positions are modified (open interest, entry price, collateral).

patterns.title: 4. Order Execution Vulnerabilities
patterns.rootCause: Order execution uses future prices, but order updates reset the execution window, allowing users to repeatedly delay execution until prices are favorable.

patterns.title: 6. LP Vault Exploitation
patterns.rootCause: Classic first-depositor inflation in perp LP vault

patterns.title: 7. Bad Debt & Protocol Insolvency
patterns.rootCause: No path to clear bad debt — accrues indefinitely

patterns.title: Section 1: Utilization Formula Errors
patterns.rootCause: The standard Compound utilization formula is `borrows / (cash + borrows - reserves)`. When `reserves` doesn't represent tokens actually held by the market (e.g., in ERC721 markets where reserves count

patterns.title: Section 2: Chain-Specific Constant Errors
patterns.rootCause: Compound-forked interest rate models use `blocksPerYear` to convert annualized rates to per-block rates. When deployed on chains with different block times (BNB Chain: 3s, Ethereum: 12-15s, L2s: 2s),

patterns.title: Section 3: Utilization Manipulation & Rate Gaming
patterns.rootCause: Interest rate models that use spot (instantaneous) balances for utilization calculation are vulnerable to manipulation. Attackers can temporarily alter pool state (via flash loans, large deposits/with

patterns.title: Section 4: Debt Token Scaling & Rate Accounting Errors
patterns.rootCause: When interest rate calculations use `debtToken.totalSupply()` or `debtToken.scaledTotalSupply()` as the borrows input, scaling mismatches between the debt token's internal representation and the actua

patterns.title: Section 5: Rate Model DoS & System Halt
patterns.rootCause: When interest rates grow beyond expected bounds (due to utilization >100%, compounding errors, or external rate caps), the `accrueInterest()` function can revert, halting all market operations (borrow

patterns.title: 1. Reentrancy via onERC721Received / safeMint Callbacks
patterns.rootCause: updateDistribution lacks noReenter modifier

patterns.title: 2. Residual Allowance & Vault Deposit Exploitation
patterns.rootCause: 'from' can be any address with residual allowance

patterns.title: 3. Marketplace Fee Bypass & Royalty Calculation Errors
patterns.rootCause: Royalty collected even when recipient is address(0)

patterns.title: 4. NFT Rental Griefing via Callback Revert & Blocklist
patterns.rootCause: safeTransferFrom allows lender callback to revert entire settlement

patterns.title: 5. Gnosis Safe Guard Bypass via Fallback Handler
patterns.rootCause: Guard doesn't check setFallbackHandler

patterns.title: 6. Auction Manipulation, Frontrunning & Debt Shortfall
patterns.rootCause: Shortfall burned without tracking for future auctions

patterns.title: 7. NFT Bridge One-Way Lock & Migration Burn
patterns.rootCause: newVault can be address(0) with invalid proposalId

patterns.title: 8. Merkle Criteria Resolution & Order Matching Bypass
patterns.rootCause: tokenId used directly as leaf — collides with intermediate hashes

patterns.title: 9. Flash Loan & Pool Token Theft
patterns.rootCause: Caller chooses token address — can be exploit contract

patterns.title: 10. NFT Wrap/Unwrap Airdrop & ID Swap Exploitation
patterns.rootCause: Immediate unwrap allows NFT ID swapping and airdrop theft
